coredata.dc:title: Dual-emission rare-earth fluorescent nanomaterials for ratiometric and visual detection of N-acetylneuraminic acid and applications in information encryption and anti-counterfeiting
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Jiaxin
Gu, Qingyang
Er, Xinyu
Sun, Jia
Zhao, Lixia
Qin, Rui
Jin, Haibo
coredata.dc:description: N-acetylneuraminic acid (NANA) can be used as a biomarker for many types of cancers. Currently, there are various methods for detecting NANA but showing some shortcomings that limit the real-time diagnosis of cancer. In contrast, fluorescence analysis has obvious advantages such as low cost, fast response time, and easy operation, and it also enables visual detection for real-time cancer monitoring. Therefore, the establishment of an efficient and rapid detection method is essential for the early prevention and treatment of the disease. Based on the properties of layered rare-earth hydroxide (LRH), we synthesized a dual-emission fluorescent material (NDC/SDS-LEuH), and further fabricated a fluorescent nanoprobe (ANP) for the detection of NANA. The probe has the advantages of high sensitivity (LOD = 32.9 μM) and high selectivity with fast response. During the sensing process, the dual emission of the probe shows opposite changes due to the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) effect and the interaction between NANA and the probe. The color changes of the system can be observed under UV irradiation. Therefore, a visual platform was developed to detect NANA with a LOD of 0.09 mM. In addition, a probe hydrogel was prepared, which can be applied in the anti-counterfeiting to improve the difficulty of counterfeiting and the security of anti-counterfeiting. The probe achieves ratiometric fluorescence detection of NANA, which reduces background interference and improves the accuracy of detection. A visual detection platform was fabricated to realize the real-time detection. In addition, the prepared probe hydrogel showed the potential applications in anti-counterfeiting, which provided new ideas for the design and application of anti-counterfeiting materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence
Nanoprobe
N-acetylneuraminic acid
Visual detection
Information encryption and anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology, structure and properties of materials
Fluorescent properties of solid-state composite and ANP
Sensing mechanism of ANP to NANA
Detection of NANA in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) XRD patterns of Cl-LEuH (a) and NDC/SDS-LEuH (b, b′); (B) FT-IR spectra of Cl-LEuH (a), SDS (b), H2NDC (c) and NDC/SDS-LEuH (d); (C)SEM image of Cl-LEuH (a, b) and NDC/SDS-LEuH (c, d); (D) Element mapping diagram of NDC/SDS-LEuH.

(A) XPS spectra of NDC/SDS-LEuH; (B) XPS spectra of C 1s in NDC/SDS-LEuH; (C) XPS spectra of O 1s in NDC/SDS-LEuH; (D) XPS spectra of Eu 3d in NDC/SDS-LEuH; (E) XPS spectra of Cl-LEuH (a) and NDC/SDS-LEuH (b) at Eu 3d; (F) XPS spectra of Cl-LEuH (a) and NDC/SDS-LEuH (b) at O 1s.
Solid-state excitation and emission spectra of Cl-LEuH (A), H2NDC (B), NDC/SDS-LEuH (C). (a: the excitation spectra; b: the emission spectra).

(A) The fluorescence responses of ANP to different concentration of NANA; (B) Fluorescence intensity of ANP at 425 nm and 613 nm with increasing concentration of NANA; (C) Linearity of NANA (0.8–5 mM) concentration versus I 425/I 613 under 280 nm excitation; (D) Colorimetric plots of ANP with different NANA concentrations; (E) Selectivity and anti-interference of ANP against kinds of interfering substances.

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of ANP before and after the addition of NANA; (B) LUMO and HOMO energy level of NANA and H2NDC; Fluorescence lifetimes of ANP at 613 nm (C) and 425 nm (D) with and without NANA (λ ex = 280 nm).
(A) Images of ANP solutions containing different concentrations of NANA under an UV lamp (λ ex = 254 nm); (B) Linear relationship of B/R with NANA concentration.
Information encryption and anti-counterfeiting applications of hydrogels.
Schematic diagram of NDC/SDS-LEuH preparation and NANA detection.
Detection of NANA in fetal bovine serum.

head.ce:title: Dual-emission rare-earth fluorescent nanomaterials for ratiometric and visual detection of N-acetylneuraminic acid and applications in information encryption and anti-counterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of New Materials and Chemical Engineering, Beijing Institute of Petrochemical Technology, Beijing, 102617, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of New Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of New Materials and Chemical Engineering, Beijing Institute of Petrochemical Technology, Beijing, 102617, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Key Laboratory of Fuels Cleaning and Advanced Catalytic Emission Reduction Technology, Beijing, 102617, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory of Fuels Cleaning and Advanced Catalytic Emission Reduction Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Key Laboratory of Fuels Cleaning and Advanced Catalytic Emission Reduction Technology, Beijing, 102617, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of New Materials and Chemical Engineering, Beijing Institute of Petrochemical Technology, Beijing, 102617, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of New Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N-acetylneuraminic acid (NANA) can be used as a biomarker for many types of cancers. Currently, there are various methods for detecting NANA but showing some shortcomings that limit the real-time diagnosis of cancer. In contrast, fluorescence analysis has obvious advantages such as low cost, fast response time, and easy operation, and it also enables visual detection for real-time cancer monitoring. Therefore, the establishment of an efficient and rapid detection method is essential for the early prevention and treatment of the disease. Based on the properties of layered rare-earth hydroxide (LRH), we synthesized a dual-emission fluorescent material (NDC/SDS-LEuH), and further fabricated a fluorescent nanoprobe (ANP) for the detection of NANA. The probe has the advantages of high sensitivity (LOD = 32.9 μM) and high selectivity with fast response. During the sensing process, the dual emission of the probe shows opposite changes due to the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) effect and the interaction between NANA and the probe. The color changes of the system can be observed under UV irradiation. Therefore, a visual platform was developed to detect NANA with a LOD of 0.09 mM. In addition, a probe hydrogel was prepared, which can be applied in the anti-counterfeiting to improve the difficulty of counterfeiting and the security of anti-counterfeiting. The probe achieves ratiometric fluorescence detection of NANA, which reduces background interference and improves the accuracy of detection. A visual detection platform was fabricated to realize the real-time detection. In addition, the prepared probe hydrogel showed the potential applications in anti-counterfeiting, which provided new ideas for the design and application of anti-counterfeiting materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Ovarian cancer is one of the most malignant tumors, with the highest morbidity and mortality rates among women. It represents a significant threat to the health of women worldwide. It is the fifth leading cause of death in women and the most fatal malignancy in gynecology. Therefore, it has been called to be the "king of gynecological cancers" [1]. Ovarian cancer is a seriously underestimated invisible and fatal disease. Most ovarian cancer patients are diagnosed at a later stage due to the lack of reliable diagnostic methods in the initial stages [2–4]. Therefore, early detection is of great significance in the reduction of mortality in patients with ovarian cancer. Scientific studies have proved that the level of N-acetylneuraminic acid (NANA) in the body is closely related to ovarian cancer and can be considered as an effective biomarker of ovarian cancer. In addition, NANA levels can be a valuable tool in the early diagnosis of lung cancer, which has been linked to the risk of developing lung cancer. Therefore, the detection of NANA concentration can be an effective aid in the early diagnosis of lung cancer [5,6]. NANA concentration has also been associated with Alzheimer's disease (AD) and can be used as a biomarker [7,8].

To date, several analytical methods are being applied to detect NANA [9–13]. Moreover, the reported methods have some drawbacks, including complex operation, expensive instruments, and susceptibility to interference from background fluorescence, probe concentration, and the circumstances of detecting, resulting in inconspicuous fluorescence color changes and inconvenience of visual detection, which greatly limits real-time diagnosis of cancer. In contrast to the traditional analytical methods, fluorescence analysis is regarded as a promising approach, which has obvious advantages such as low cost, fast response time, and easy operation. Cao et al. [14] prepared the fluorescent sensor UiO-CPBA by modifying UiO-66-NH2 using 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid (4-CPBA). The detection of sialic acid (SA) was achieved with a limit of detection of 0.15 μM due to photoinduced electron transfer (PET). Xu et al. [15] prepared functionalized carbon dots by using 3-pyridineboronic acid as a carbon source for detecting NANA with a limit of detection of 54 μM.

Various materials, including carbon dots and nanoparticles, have been used as fluorescent probes [16,17]. Layered rare-earth hydroxide (LRH) is an anionic and emerging functional material made up of interlayer anions and actively charged layers [18]. LRH possesses both the unique luminescence properties [19,20] of rare-earth ions, such as large Stokes shifts and long fluorescence lifetimes [21], as well as the properties of layered materials, contributing to a multi-application in fluorescence sensing. Liu et al. [22] synthesized a layered europium hydroxide to achieve selective detection of MnO4 − or Cr2O7 2− by fluorescence quenching.

Ratiometric fluorescent probes [23,24] can reduce background interference and provide higher sensitivity and accuracy [25] than most single-emission fluorescent probes, as well as exhibit significant color changes to the target analyte, resulting in the design of visual sensing platforms and the improvement of detection efficiencies. Our research group [24–29] has also been focusing on developing fluorescent probe materials and obtained a variety of fluorescent composites with good sensing of ions, antibiotics, and biomarkers. Besides, rare-earth ions can be applied in the field of message encryption and anti-counterfeiting to improve the complexity of message encryption and the security of anti-counterfeiting [30,31].

Eu3+, as the most representative fluorescent ion among rare-earth ions, shows excellent fluorescence properties and wide application prospects, which has been widely used in biomolecular detection, medical diagnosis, environmental detection. The abundant carboxyl and hydroxyl groups in the NANA molecules can form certain interactions with Eu3+. In addition, the fluorescence color changes are more obvious during the visualization detection due to the unique fluorescence property of Eu3+. In this paper, composite materials were synthesized using layered europium hydroxide (LEuH) as a precursor, and 1,4-naphthoic acid (H2NDC) and sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) as intercalating substances. The composites were exfoliated into nanosheets in a formamide solution, and dual-emission fluorescent nanoprobe (ANP) was prepared for the ratiometric detection of NANA (Scheme 1 ). The ANP showed two emission centers including blue emission of H2NDC and red emission of Eu3+. In the presence of NANA, the fluorescence of H2NDC increased significantly, while the red fluorescence of Eu3+ gradually decreased, enabling the ratiometric detection of NANA. A visual sensing platform was designed based on the obvious color changes combined with a smartphone. Additionally, the probe hydrogels are prepared for the potential application in the field of anti-counterfeiting.

The detail procedure of the preparation of EuCl3·6H2O and Cl-LEuH precursor is in the supplementary material. The nanocomposites (NDC/SDS-LEuH) were prepared as follows: An aqueous solution was formed by dissolving SDS (0.63 mmol), H2NDC (0.63 mmol), and NaOH (1.26 mmol) in 80 mL of deionized water. 0.1 g of Cl-LEuH was dispersed in the above solution and transferred to a reactor (70 °C/24 h). NDC/SDS-LEuH composites were obtained by filtering, washing, and drying under vacuum (45 °C/12 h).

NDC/SDS-LEuH (30 mg) was dispersed into 50 mL mixture of formamide and water (formamide: water = 3:2), and the colloidal suspension was held at 45 °C for 24 h. The obtained nanosheets can function as the fluorescent probe, marked as ANP. Detection conditions: the concentration of ANP was 0.6 mg/mL, the solvent of the assay system was water, and the pH was 6.8.
Different concentrations of NANA (2 mL) and 1 mL of ANP were mixed well and placed in a cuvette and the fluorescence spectra (350–750 nm) were measured under λ ex = 280 nm.

The preparation method of the hydrogel was improved based on the literature [32]. 0.2 g of agar powder was dissolved in 20 mL of boiling deionized water and stirred continuously until the powder was completely dissolved, and the solution became clear and transparent to stop heating, and a blank hydrogel was obtained after cooling.

The preparation of the probe hydrogel was similar to that of blank hydrogel, specifically, 0.2 g of agar powder was dissolved in 10 mL of boiling deionized water with constant stirring until the powder was completely dissolved and the solution became clarified and transparent to stop the heating, and then 10 mL of ANP was added immediately, stirred rapidly and evenly, and cooled down to room temperature to obtain the probe hydrogel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology, structure and properties of materials
ce:section.ce:para: Successful synthesis of the composites was demonstrated using a variety of characterization methods. Fig. 1 A shows the XRD patterns of Cl-LEuH and NDC/SDS-LEuH. Cl-LEuH (Fig. 1A–a) exhibited sharp (00l) diffractions at 0.84 nm and 0.42 nm, indicating a layer spacing (d basal) of 0.84 nm, as reported in the literature [33]. The diffraction peaks of NDC/SDS-LEuH composites (Fig. 1A–b, b′) appeared at 2.62, 1.28, 0.85, 0.64, and 0.43 nm, with a d basal of 2.62 nm. The composites were successfully prepared, as evidenced by the significant increase in the interlayer spacing.

To understand the structure of the synthesized materials, FT-IR analyses were carried out for Cl-LEuH, SDS, H2NDC, and NDC/SDS-LEuH. The absorption at 1632 cm−1 is ascribed to the bending vibration of –OH in Cl-LEuH, and the absorptions at 1134 cm−1, 619 cm−1, and 518 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of Eu–O [34] (Fig. 1B–a). The absorptions of SDS at 1223 cm−1, 1086 cm−1, and 1020 cm−1 (Fig. 1B–b) are the stretching vibrations of S–O [35]. For H2NDC, 1693 cm−1 is the absorption belonging to the free –COOH group [36]. After intercalating the organic guest, some absorptions can be found to be slightly red-shifted (1223 cm−1→1234 cm−1, 1084 cm−1→1070 cm−1), and the absorptions of –COOH are weakened, which indicates that Eu3+ binds to the –COOH groups through coordination, suggesting that the composites were prepared successfully. In addition, the XRD and FTIR of ANP were also determined. Comparing with the XRD patterns of NDC/SDS-LEuH, there was no obvious layer diffraction of ANP and only one broad diffraction peak in the range of 20∼30° caused by scattering of formamide (Fig. S1A). In addition to the peaks at 1606 and 1681 cm−1 in the IR pattern, a new peak appeared at 1718 cm−1, which was a blueshift of the carboxyl peak of NDC/SDS-LEuH (Fig. S1B).

The morphology of Cl-LEuH and NDC/SDS-LEuH was investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The Cl-LEuH precursors (Fig. 1C–a, b) showed flakes or flower-like aggregates assembled from flakes, while the composites (Fig. 1C–c, d) maintained the morphology of the precursors. These morphological features were attributed to the incorporation of anionic surfactant (SDS), implying good layer aggregation during the synthesis [37]. The elemental mapping diagram of the material after organic intercalation is shown in Fig. 1D. The presence of S elements and the uniform distribution of C, O, and Eu in NDC/SDS-LEuH confirms the presence of organic compounds in the interlayer.

Elemental analyses of Cl-LEuH and NDC/SDS-LEuH were conducted using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) as shown in Fig. 2 . The wide XPS profile (Fig. 2A) demonstrated the presence of the elements C, O, S, and Eu in NDC/SDS-LEuH. In this case, the C 1s profile of NDC/SDS-LEuH was decomposed into two peaks, CO and C–C (Fig. 2B), and the O 1s profile was decomposed into two peaks. The CO and O–H peaks (Fig. 2C) were attributed to the carboxyl group present in the H2NDC [38]. The Eu 3d profile exhibited a single Eu–O peak (Fig. 2D). In comparison with Cl-LEuH, the Eu 3d peak of NDC/SDS-LEuH exhibited a shift from 1137.2 eV to 1140.2 eV (Fig. 2E), and the peak of O 1s shifted from 533.95 eV to 534.05 eV (Fig. 2F), which further confirmed the coordination between Eu3+ and carboxyl groups, and the composites were successfully prepared.

Analyzing the thermogravimetric (TG) data, it can be observed that the weight loss rate of NDC/SDS-LEuH (Fig. S2b) was much lower than that of H2NDC (Fig. S2c) and slightly higher than that of Cl-LEuH (Fig. S2a), indicating that the thermal stability of the composites was enhanced by the intercalation.

A clear Tyndall effect can be seen in the exfoliated colloid (Fig. S3 inset). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) image showed that the thickness of the ANP nanosheets was about 1.17 nm (Fig. S3), confirming the monolayer structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent properties of solid-state composite and ANP
ce:section.ce:para: The solid-state luminescence properties of Cl-LEuH, organic H2NDC and composites were investigated, and the characteristic peaks of Eu3+ could be observed in the excitation and emission spectra of Cl-LEuH (Fig. 3 A). The maximum emission peak of H2NDC and NDC/SDS-LEuH appeared at 482 nm (Figs. 3B) and 450 nm (Fig. 3C), respectively. This results suggested that the emission at 450 nm of NDC/SDS-LEuH was mainly based on the S1→S0 transition of H2NDC [39].

The luminescence properties of ANP were further investigated at room temperature. ANP showed the emission of H2NDC at 425 nm, and the characteristic 5D0→7FJ (J = 1–4) transitions of Eu3+ at 590, 613, 649, and 693 nm (λ ex = 280 nm), and the maximum emission was observed at 613 nm (Fig. S4). When NANA was added to the ANP, the fluorescence of H2NDC increased but that of Eu3+ decreased (Fig. S5). Stability is an important indicator for the fluorescent probe, so the emission spectra of ANP were measured at different time intervals (0, 24, 48, 72 h) (Fig. S6). The fluorescence intensity was almost unchanged compared to the original fluorescence intensity of ANP, indicating a good fluorescence stability.

The fluorescence of H2NDC (425 nm) in ANP increased significantly with increasing NANA concentration, while the fluorescence of Eu3+ gradually decreased (Fig. 4 A and B). In addition, the fluorescence responses (I 425/I 613) showed a good linear relationship with concentration (0.8–5 mM) and the linear equation: y = 0.155x + 0.456 (R
2 = 0.996) (Fig. 4C). Further calculations showed that the detection limit of ANP for NANA was 32.9 μM (3σ/k), which was much lower than the level of NANA in lung cancer patients [9] (3 mM), and the probe showed a better detection performance compared with other fluorescent probes (Table S1). The chromaticity coordinates of the CIE moved from the red region to the blue region (Fig. 4D), indicating that visual detection of NANA was expected. In order to investigate the selectivity and anti-interference of the probe, common ions and interferents were added to the system. Common interferents include NaCl, KCl, Na2SO4, Urea, Mannitol (Man), Galactose (Gal), Sucrose (Glc), and Glucose (Glu). As shown in Fig. 4E, only NANA resulted in a significant increase in the fluorescence response (I 425/I 613), demonstrating the high selectivity of ANP to NANA. The other substances had little influence on the fluorescence response, indicating good anti-interference properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of ANP to NANA
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing mechanism of ANP to NANA was further investigated in detail. Fig. 5 A shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of ANP before and after adding NANA. The absorption of ANP was not enhanced in the presence of NANA, which eliminated the possibility of absorption enhancement effect (ACE). To investigate the possibility of photo-induced electron transfer (PET), the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) levels were calculated on Gaussian software using the B3LYP/6-31G method of density-functional theory (DFT). When the LUMO energy level of the donor was higher than that of the acceptor, the donor can transfer electrons to the acceptor [40,41]. From the calculations in Fig. 5B, the LUMO energy level of NANA was higher than that of the ligand H2NDC, resulting in the transfer of electrons from NANA to H2NDC, which leaded to the enhancement of fluorescence intensity at 425 nm. Besides, certain interactions of NANA with Eu3+ may be formed due to the abundance of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups in the NANA molecule, which may disrupt the energy transfer between H2NDC and Eu3+, resulting in the luminescence quenching of Eu3+ and the enhancement of ligand fluorescence [42,43]. In addition, the fluorescence lifetimes of H2NDC and Eu3+ were determined (Fig. 5C and D). After the addition of NANA, the fluorescence lifetimes of Eu3+ were decreased from 214.20 μs to 207.72 μs, and the fluorescence lifetimes of the ligand were increased from 0.93 μs to 1.23 μs. In summary, the sensing mechanism of ANP to NANA was attributed to the PET effect and the interaction between NANA and ANP.

Based on the excellent fluorescent properties of ANP, the hydrogel was prepared and applied to the field of anti-counterfeiting. Pour the uncooled blank hydrogel into a 96-well plate to form the letters (F, X) and leave until completely cooled. Pour the uncooled probe hydrogel into a 96-well plate to form the letters (Z, L) and leave until completely cooled. Under daylight, there is no difference between the four letters, which are all milky white (Fig. 7 ). However, under 254 nm UV light, the letters Z and L can be observed to turn pinkish red. This interesting phenomenon suggests that ANP hydrogel can reveal different colors under specific UV lights, thus making it more difficult to be counterfeited, and significantly increasing the sophistication and security of message encryption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of NANA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the superior detection of NANA by ANP, different concentrations of NANA were spiked in fetal bovine serum (Table 1 ) to assess the feasibility of the practical application of this fluorescent nanoprobe. The recoveries of NANA in real samples were 100.3 %–103.0 % and the relative standard deviations (RSD) of 1.33 %–2.74 %, which verified the reliability of the NANA detection.

In summary, a dual-emission fluorescent nanoprobe (ANP) was fabricated based on the fluorescence properties of Eu3+ and its interaction with NANA. The probe shows high sensitivity (LOD = 32.9 μM), selectivity and fast response to the detection of NANA. When NANA was added to the probe, the fluorescence at 425 nm was enhanced obviously, and the fluorescence at 613 nm was gradually decreased, which was due to the PET effect and the interaction between NANA and H2NDC. More importantly, the color changes caused by NANA was easily observed under UV irradiation, promoting the fabrication of a visual platform to detect NANA with the LOD of 0.09 mM. Based on the excellent fluorescence properties of ANP, the probe hydrogel was prepared to be applied in the field of anti-counterfeiting. The probe hydrogel improves the difficulty of counterfeiting and the security of anti-counterfeiting, and this strategy offers a new idea for the design and application of anti-counterfeiting materials.

Jiaxin Wei: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Qingyang Gu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Xinyu Er: Validation, Formal analysis. Jia Sun: Investigation. Lixia Zhao: Formal analysis. Rui Qin: Software. Haibo Jin: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Beijing Natural Science Foundation (2232051), the Young Elite Scientists Sponsorship Program by BAST (BYESS2023258), and the Classified Development Projects of Beijing Municipal Institutions (11000023T000002199202).
